did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes MSFINet, a deep learning network for skin lesion image segmentation. It utilizes AI methods including a Channel Interaction Transformer (CIT) module, a Channel Refinement Attention (CRA) module, a Multi-Kernel Dual Branch Aggregation (MKDA) module, and a Boundary Difference Optimization (BDO) module. The model was trained and evaluated on dermatological datasets (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, and PH2) to segment melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning architecture named MSFINet for skin lesion image segmentation. It introduces specific modules (CIT, CRA, MKDA, BDO), presents experimental results on three datasets (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, PH2) with quantitative metrics (DSC, ACC, SP, SE), and includes ablation studies. Although published in the IJCNN conference proceedings, it presents original experimental results and a new algorithm, fitting the definition of a research paper.

title: MSFINet: Multi-Scale Feature Interaction Fusion Network for Skin Lesion Image Segmentation
journal: 2025 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN)
brief_summary: This study proposes MSFINet, a multi-scale feature interaction fusion network designed to address the challenges of skin lesion image segmentation, such as blurred boundaries and hair occlusion. The architecture incorporates a Channel Interaction Transformer (CIT) module, a Channel Refinement Attention (CRA) module, a Multi-Kernel Dual Branch Aggregation (MKDA) module, and a Boundary Difference Optimization (BDO) module to enhance feature extraction and boundary sensitivity. The model was evaluated on three public dermatological datasets: ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, and PH2. Performance was assessed using metrics including Dice Coefficient (DSC), Accuracy (ACC), Sensitivity (SE), and Specificity (SP). The results demonstrated that MSFINet outperformed existing methods, achieving DSC score improvements of 1.71%, 1.36%, and 0.87% over the suboptimal networks on the respective datasets. These findings indicate that MSFINet effectively improves segmentation accuracy for melanoma detection tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on segmenting melanoma lesions. The abstract states 'Segmentation of lesion areas in dermoscopic images is key to diagnosing melanoma.' The introduction discusses melanoma as a tumor resulting from malignant transformation of melanocytes. The experiments use datasets (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, PH2) which contain melanoma images for segmentation tasks.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise binary mask of melanoma lesion areas in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin Lesion Image Segmentation'. The model predicts semantic probability masks for lesion vs. normal skin pixels. Evaluation metrics include Dice Coefficient (DSC), Accuracy (ACC), Sensitivity (SE), and Specificity (SP), which are standard for segmentation tasks. The output is a segmentation mask, not a class label or continuous regression value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset for skin lesion image segmentation containing original dermatoscopic images and their corresponding annotations. Images are 576 × 576 pixels in size.

use.validation: Used for validation of the MSFINet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the MSFINet model; performance metrics (DSC, ACC, SP, SE) were reported.

datasets_used.description: A public dataset for skin lesion image segmentation containing original dermatoscopic images and their corresponding annotations. Images are 576 × 576 pixels in size.

use.training: Used for training the MSFINet model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the MSFINet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the MSFINet model; performance metrics (DSC, ACC, SP, SE) were reported.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking. Contains original images and their corresponding annotations. Images are 768 × 560 pixels in size, preprocessed and uniformly resized to 224 × 224 pixels for model input.

use.training: Used for training the MSFINet model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the MSFINet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the MSFINet model; performance metrics (DSC, ACC, SP, SE) were reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (DSC, ACC, SP, SE) and percentage improvements over baseline models without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT) used as the encoder module
architecture_used.input_size: 224 × 224 pixels (for PH2 dataset); 576 × 576 pixels (for ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets, resized during preprocessing)

architecture_details.encoder: Pretrained Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT) generating multilevel feature maps {Li | i ∈ (1, 2, 3, 4)}

mkda_details.operation: Depthwise (DW) convolution with expansion factors {1, 2, 5, 7} in parallel

bdo_details.mechanism: Dual-path boundary fusion scheme using boundary difference operation on semantic probability masks

components: Linear transforms for Queries (Q), Keys (K), Values (V)

training_details.loss_function: Hybrid loss (Lbce + Ldice) with weights λ0=1, λ1=0.5, λ2=0.5

classification_or_output_head.details: Decoder outputs semantic probability masks for lesion vs. normal skin pixels

preprocessing_techniques: Uniform resizing of PH2 dataset images from 768 × 560 pixels to 224 × 224 pixels to suit model input requirements
Preprocessing of PH2 dataset consistent with previous work [5]
conclusions: The proposed MSFINet network effectively addresses the challenges of skin lesion image segmentation, specifically blurred boundaries and hair occlusion, by integrating a Channel Interaction Transformer (CIT), Channel Refinement Attention (CRA), Multi-Kernel Dual Branch Aggregation (MKDA), and Boundary Difference Optimization (BDO) modules.

MSFINet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on three public dermatological datasets (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, and PH2), achieving Dice Coefficient (DSC) score improvements of 1.71%, 1.36%, and 0.87% respectively over the suboptimal networks.

The integration of the MKDA and BDO modules provides complementary performance benefits, with MKDA enhancing global contextual information and resistance to interference, while BDO improves boundary sensitivity and detail optimization.

The proposed modules (CIT, CRA, MKDA, BDO) are mutually beneficial, as ablation studies demonstrate that removing any module leads to performance degradation, confirming their individual importance in the network architecture.

A hybrid loss function combining Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) and Dice Loss with weights λ0=1, λ1=0.5, and λ2=0.5 yields the optimal segmentation performance compared to using either loss function alone or different weight combinations.

MSFINet achieves a favorable balance between computational complexity and accuracy; while its parameter count (47.29M) is higher than some pure CNNs, it is lower than many Transformer-based models, and its FLOPs (11.79G) are competitive, resulting in middle-range training time consumption with superior accuracy.

The model demonstrates robustness against interfering factors such as hair occlusion and pigmented patches, producing clearer and more complete segmentation masks for tiny lesions and blurred boundaries compared to other networks.

While MSFINet achieved optimal DSC scores across all datasets, its Sensitivity (SE) was suboptimal on ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets; however, this is attributed to the contradictory nature of Specificity (SP) and SE metrics, where MSFINet's significantly higher SP scores indicate strong overall competitiveness.
important_remarks: The study lacks formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values); performance improvements are reported as absolute percentage increases over suboptimal baselines without confidence intervals.
The model's Sensitivity (SE) was suboptimal on the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets, although this is attributed to the trade-off with high Specificity (SP) scores.
External validation is limited to three public datasets (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, PH2); no clinical deployment or multi-center external validation is reported.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images) and was preprocessed by resizing to 224x224 pixels, which may introduce bias compared to the native resolution of other datasets.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed train/validation/test splits for the public datasets, relying on standard challenge protocols.
Computational complexity is moderate (47.29M parameters, 11.79G FLOPs), which is higher than some pure CNNs but competitive with Transformer-based models.
